skill.name: grasshopper-workflow
skill.description: Automates Grasshopper parametric modeling workflows through local Python tools and a Grasshopper MCP connection. It parses MMD design files, generates placement plans, creates components, connects nodes, sets parameters, and groups components.
skill.summary: Build Grasshopper definitions from MMD files and placement plans through a local MCP workflow.
skill.author: AmemiyaLai

security_audit.summary: AI review did not confirm the static scanner high-risk malware indicators. Most external command, weak crypto, C2, reconnaissance, and entropy hits are false positives caused by Markdown examples, Grasshopper command names, and UUID-like component GUIDs. The remaining risk is medium because the skill includes Python scripts that read and write local files and send user-provided modeling commands to a local Grasshopper MCP socket.

medium_findings.title: Local Grasshopper Command Channel
medium_findings.description: The client opens a TCP socket to the configured Grasshopper MCP server and sends serialized command objects. This is expected for the skill, but untrusted placement data can create, connect, delete, or modify Grasshopper document components through that local service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The socket destination defaults to localhost and supports a legitimate Grasshopper workflow. Confidence is high that commands are sent locally, but there is no evidence of malicious outbound exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Local File Writes
medium_findings.description: Several CLI commands write JSON output to paths provided by the user, and utility functions update placement or component map files. This can overwrite local files if a user supplies an unsafe path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and use user-controlled or caller-provided paths. The behavior is functional rather than malicious, but it needs normal local-file caution.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Local Import Fallback
medium_findings.description: The CLI modifies sys.path and can load a local __init__.py with importlib when normal imports fail. This supports direct script execution, but it can execute code from the local skill directory if that directory is modified.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import path manipulation is visible and legitimate for a CLI helper. Risk depends on the local directory contents being trusted, and no remote import source was found.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown inline code, fenced examples, and CLI documentation. No subprocess, os.system, shell popen, or equivalent command execution was found in the executable scripts reviewed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are documentation or examples, and script search found no shell execution primitives. This strongly supports a false-positive verdict for external command execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are UUID-like Grasshopper component GUIDs and GUID mapping values, not cryptographic functions or hashing algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code identifies these values as component GUIDs. No encryption, hashing, or password storage code is present at the cited locations.

low_findings.title: C2 and Reconnaissance Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword matches come from Grasshopper command names such as connect_components, and reconnaissance matches come from component queries and document error checks. No evidence found of system inventory, persistence, or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are domain-specific modeling operations. The reviewed code targets Grasshopper document state rather than host system reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Socket-Based Command Dispatch
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill sends JSON commands over a TCP socket to the configured Grasshopper MCP service. Users should run this only against a trusted local Grasshopper session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly opens a socket and sends serialized commands. The destination is local by default, so this is a controlled integration risk rather than confirmed abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local JSON File Mutation
dangerous_patterns.description: The utilities read and rewrite JSON files such as placement plans and component ID maps. The code does not restrict output paths to the skill directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file mutation is explicit and path-based. It is intended behavior, but unsafe path choices can affect unrelated local files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Import Path Expansion
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI inserts the parent directory into sys.path before importing modules. This can execute unexpected local code if the working copy is tampered with.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is common in standalone Python CLIs. It becomes risky mainly when the skill directory or parent path is not trusted.

content.value_statement: Grasshopper workflows often require many repeated component, connection, and parameter steps. This skill provides scripts and guidance to turn MMD plans into executable Grasshopper MCP workflows.
actual_capabilities: Parse component_info.mmd files into component and connection data.
Generate placement_info.json plans from parsed MMD structures.
Create Grasshopper components through a local MCP client.
Connect generated components and set slider or vector values.
Create grouped Grasshopper components with optional colors.
Run a CLI for parsing, generation, placement, grouping, and error checks.
limitations: Requires a running Grasshopper MCP server, usually on localhost port 8080.
Works only with component formats and GUID mappings supported by the scripts.
Does not validate all design intent before changing the Grasshopper document.
Can overwrite local output files when users provide unsafe paths.

use_cases.title: Generate Parametric Layouts
use_cases.description: Convert MMD component plans into structured placement steps for a Grasshopper definition.

use_cases.title: Automate Repetitive Grasshopper Setup
use_cases.description: Create components, connect nodes, set sliders, and group related model sections with less manual work.

use_cases.title: Review MCP Modeling Workflows
use_cases.description: Inspect generated placement plans before executing them against a local Grasshopper session.

prompt_templates.title: Explain The Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how this Grasshopper workflow skill turns component_info.mmd into a Grasshopper definition. Keep the steps concise.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Placement Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill references to plan how I should generate placement_info.json from my component_info.mmd file.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Failed Connections
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Grasshopper workflow failure and identify likely causes in component IDs, parameter names, or connection order.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Full Automation Run
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this placement workflow before execution. Check component creation, connection dependencies, slider ranges, grouping, and local file risks.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to turn an MMD component map into a Grasshopper definition.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step workflow covering MMD parsing, placement plan generation, component creation, connection execution, slider setup, and grouping.

output_examples.input: A user reports missing component IDs during connection execution.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist that checks component creation results, saved ID maps, connection source and target names, and Grasshopper MCP responses.

output_examples.input: A user wants to run the CLI safely on a project file.
output_examples.output: A cautious execution plan that confirms the MCP server, backs up outputs, reviews generated commands, and uses project-local paths.

best_practices: Review generated placement plans before executing them in Grasshopper.
Use project-local input and output paths to avoid overwriting unrelated files.
Run the MCP server only for trusted local Grasshopper sessions.
anti_patterns: Do not execute placement files from untrusted sources without review.
Do not point output arguments at important existing files.
Do not assume every Grasshopper component name has a supported GUID mapping.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It helps parse MMD files, generate placement plans, and drive Grasshopper component workflows through local Python scripts.

faq.question: Does it require Rhino or Grasshopper?
faq.answer: Yes. Execution requires a local Grasshopper MCP server connected to a Grasshopper session.

faq.question: Can it create components automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts can send add_component commands with GUIDs and canvas positions to the MCP server.

faq.question: Can it change local files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can write placement plans, parsed outputs, error reports, and component ID maps.

faq.question: Are the static high-risk alerts confirmed?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed high-risk alerts are mostly false positives from GUIDs, Markdown examples, and Grasshopper command names.

faq.question: What should users review before running it?
faq.answer: Users should review generated placement commands, output paths, component GUIDs, and the target MCP server.
